clusters.title: Carb-heavy foods drove weight gain without more calories by lowering energy burn
clusters.summary: Bread is a staple that has sustained civilizations over centuries and is intrinsically etched into the very grains of society. However, as obesity rates continue to rise, it may now be time to retire this dependency in modern diets.

items.title: Carb-heavy foods drove weight gain without more calories by lowering energy burn
items.description: Bread is a staple that has sustained civilizations over centuries and is intrinsically etched into the very grains of society. However, as obesity rates continue to rise, it may now be time to retire this dependency in modern diets.

items.title: Scientists discover why bread can cause weight gain without extra calories
items.description: Bread and other carbohydrate staples may be doing more than just filling plates—they could be quietly reshaping metabolism. In a surprising twist, researchers found that mice strongly preferred carbs like bread, rice, and wheat, abandoning their regular diet entirely. Even without eating more calories, they gained weight and body fat, not because they overate, but because their bodies burned less energy.

clusters.title: AI spots melanoma risk patterns in 6 million adults up to five years early
clusters.summary: Health care registry data can show early risk patterns for melanoma skin cancer, according to a study from the University of Gothenburg. Using AI, it is possible to identify small groups within the population that have a significantly higher risk of developing melanoma within five years. The work is published in the journal Acta Dermato-Venereologica.

items.title: AI spots melanoma risk patterns in 6 million adults up to five years early

The work is published in the journal Acta Dermato-Venereologica.

clusters.title: A common nutrient could supercharge cancer treatment
clusters.summary: A common eye-health nutrient, zeaxanthin, may also help the body fight cancer more effectively. Scientists discovered it strengthens T cells and enhances the impact of immunotherapy treatments. Found in everyday vegetables and supplements, it’s safe, accessible, and shows strong potential as a cancer therapy booster.

items.title: A common nutrient could supercharge cancer treatment
items.description: A common eye-health nutrient, zeaxanthin, may also help the body fight cancer more effectively. Scientists discovered it strengthens T cells and enhances the impact of immunotherapy treatments. Found in everyday vegetables and supplements, it’s safe, accessible, and shows strong potential as a cancer therapy booster. Human trials are the next step.

clusters.title: Psychologists map out the pathways connecting sacred beliefs to better sex
clusters.summary: Viewing intimacy as a deeply spiritual or sacred experience is strongly linked to higher sexual satisfaction, especially for women. A new psychological study maps out the specific bedroom habits—like mindfulness and communication—that form the bridge between meaning and passion.

items.title: Psychologists map out the pathways connecting sacred beliefs to better sex
items.description: Viewing intimacy as a deeply spiritual or sacred experience is strongly linked to higher sexual satisfaction, especially for women. A new psychological study maps out the specific bedroom habits—like mindfulness and communication—that form the bridge between meaning and passion.

clusters.title: Your brain can trick you into liking artificial sweeteners
clusters.summary: Your brain might be quietly deciding what tastes good before you even take a sip. Researchers found that simply changing what people thought they were drinking—sugar or artificial sweetener—could dramatically shift how much they enjoyed it. When participants believed a drink had artificial sweeteners, real sugar tasted less enjoyable, but when they expected sugar, even artificially sweetened drinks became more pleasurable.

items.title: Your brain can trick you into liking artificial sweeteners

Researchers found that simply changing what people thought they were drinking—sugar or artificial sweetener—could dramatically shift how much they enjoyed it. When participants believed a drink had artificial sweeteners, real sugar tasted less enjoyable, but when they expected sugar, even artificially sweetened drinks became more pleasurable.

clusters.title: Scientists discover why your appetite suddenly disappears when you’re sick
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered how your body actually tells your brain to stop eating when you’re sick. In a new study, researchers found that specialized cells in the gut detect parasites and send signals that ultimately trigger the brain to suppress appetite. This process builds over time, explaining why you may feel fine at first but then suddenly lose interest in food as an infection takes hold.

items.title: Scientists discover why your appetite suddenly disappears when you’re sick

clusters.title: A new “magic mushroom” drug could treat depression without psychedelic hallucinations
clusters.summary: Scientists are exploring a new way to harness the medical promise of psychedelic compounds without the mind-bending side effects. Researchers created modified versions of psilocin — the active form of psilocybin from “magic mushrooms” — that still target key serotonin pathways linked to depression and other brain disorders but appear to cause far fewer psychedelic-like effects.

items.title: A new “magic mushroom” drug could treat depression without psychedelic hallucinations

clusters.title: How neighborhood amenities and infrastructure may slow cognitive decline among older immigrants
clusters.summary: Older Chinese immigrants living in neighborhoods with greater access to community amenities, services, and supportive infrastructure experienced slower cognitive decline over time, according to Rutgers Health researchers.

items.title: How neighborhood amenities and infrastructure may slow cognitive decline among older immigrants
items.description: Older Chinese immigrants living in neighborhoods with greater access to community amenities, services, and supportive infrastructure experienced slower cognitive decline over time, according to Rutgers Health researchers.

clusters.title: Artery images and nurse counseling boosted heart risk reduction over six years
clusters.summary: When health risks become visible in black and white, perspectives often shift. A study from Umeå University shows that when ultrasound images of atherosclerosis in subjects' carotid arteries are combined with a motivational dialogue, the patients are more likely to be motivated to improve their lifestyle habits and take preventive medication.

items.title: Artery images and nurse counseling boosted heart risk reduction over six years
items.description: When health risks become visible in black and white, perspectives often shift. A study from Umeå University shows that when ultrasound images of atherosclerosis in subjects' carotid arteries are combined with a motivational dialogue, the patients are more likely to be motivated to improve their lifestyle habits and take preventive medication.

clusters.title: Low doses of LSD alter emotional brain responses in people with mild depression
clusters.summary: A double-blind trial suggests that microdosing the psychedelic drug LSD can reverse blunted neurological responses to emotional rewards, potentially offering targeted biological interventions for individuals struggling with mild to moderate depression.

items.title: Low doses of LSD alter emotional brain responses in people with mild depression
items.description: A double-blind trial suggests that microdosing the psychedelic drug LSD can reverse blunted neurological responses to emotional rewards, potentially offering targeted biological interventions for individuals struggling with mild to moderate depression.

clusters.title: Why thinking hard feels bad: the emotional root of deliberation
clusters.summary: Doubt is not just a lack of information; it is an unpleasant emotional state. New psychological research reveals that this negative feeling serves as a necessary alarm bell, pushing the human brain to abandon mental shortcuts and think critically.

items.title: Why thinking hard feels bad: the emotional root of deliberation
items.description: Doubt is not just a lack of information; it is an unpleasant emotional state. New psychological research reveals that this negative feeling serves as a necessary alarm bell, pushing the human brain to abandon mental shortcuts and think critically.

clusters.title: Blood test predicts kidney failure risk to Black Americans years before onset
clusters.summary: A new blood test can identify which individuals of African ancestry carrying high-risk APOL1 gene variants are most likely to develop kidney failure, years before clinical disease becomes apparent. Findings on the new test, developed by a team from the Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania, are published in Nature Medicine.

items.title: Blood test predicts kidney failure risk to Black Americans years before onset
items.description: A new blood test can identify which individuals of African ancestry carrying high-risk APOL1 gene variants are most likely to develop kidney failure, years before clinical disease becomes apparent. Findings on the new test, developed by a team from the Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania, are published in Nature Medicine.

clusters.title: Brain study reveals hidden link between autism and ADHD
clusters.summary: Scientists are uncovering a surprising connection between autism and ADHD that goes deeper than labels. Instead of diagnoses, it’s the severity of autism-like traits that seems to shape how the brain is wired—even in children who don’t officially have autism. The study found that certain brain networks tied to thinking and social behavior stay unusually connected in kids with stronger autism symptoms, hinting at a different developmental path.

items.title: Brain study reveals hidden link between autism and ADHD

clusters.title: The surprising reason you’re so productive one day and not the next
clusters.summary: Feeling mentally “on” isn’t just in your head—it can significantly boost what you accomplish. Researchers found that sharper thinking on a given day leads people to set bigger goals and actually follow through. That edge can equal up to 40 extra minutes of productivity.

items.title: The surprising reason you’re so productive one day and not the next
items.description: Feeling mentally “on” isn’t just in your head—it can significantly boost what you accomplish. Researchers found that sharper thinking on a given day leads people to set bigger goals and actually follow through. That edge can equal up to 40 extra minutes of productivity. But push too hard for too long, and the effect reverses.

clusters.title: Can video games make kids feel better about their bodies?
clusters.summary: Researchers created a custom Roblox game to help improve body image in children. However, a new study of over 1,000 kids reveals that the game's optional educational messages failed to actually boost players' self-esteem or body satisfaction.

items.title: Can video games make kids feel better about their bodies?
items.description: Researchers created a custom Roblox game to help improve body image in children. However, a new study of over 1,000 kids reveals that the game's optional educational messages failed to actually boost players' self-esteem or body satisfaction.

clusters.title: Doing this throughout life may cut Alzheimer’s risk by 38%
clusters.summary: A lifetime of mental stimulation—like reading, writing, and learning new skills—may help protect the brain as we age. People with the highest levels of cognitive enrichment had a much lower risk of Alzheimer’s and experienced symptoms years later than those with the lowest levels.

items.title: Doing this throughout life may cut Alzheimer’s risk by 38%
items.description: A lifetime of mental stimulation—like reading, writing, and learning new skills—may help protect the brain as we age. People with the highest levels of cognitive enrichment had a much lower risk of Alzheimer’s and experienced symptoms years later than those with the lowest levels.

clusters.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
clusters.summary: After a stroke, the brain may do something surprisingly hopeful—it can “refresh” parts of itself. Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.

items.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation

clusters.title: This Mediterranean‑style diet is linked to a slower loss of brain volume as we age
clusters.summary: A recent analysis of brain scans suggests that the MIND diet—which prioritizes green vegetables, berries, and poultry—may help preserve gray matter and slow brain aging, though it remains just one piece of a larger lifestyle puzzle.

items.title: This Mediterranean‑style diet is linked to a slower loss of brain volume as we age
items.description: A recent analysis of brain scans suggests that the MIND diet—which prioritizes green vegetables, berries, and poultry—may help preserve gray matter and slow brain aging, though it remains just one piece of a larger lifestyle puzzle.

clusters.title: This “master gene” may be driving pancreatic cancer’s spread
clusters.summary: A gene called KLF5 may be a key force behind the spread of pancreatic cancer—but not in the way scientists expected. Rather than mutating DNA, it rewires how genes are turned on and off, helping tumors grow and invade new areas. Researchers found it plays a major role in metastatic cells and even controls other genes linked to cancer progression.

items.title: This “master gene” may be driving pancreatic cancer’s spread
items.description: A gene called KLF5 may be a key force behind the spread of pancreatic cancer—but not in the way scientists expected. Rather than mutating DNA, it rewires how genes are turned on and off, helping tumors grow and invade new areas. Researchers found it plays a major role in metastatic cells and even controls other genes linked to cancer progression. The discovery opens the door to new treatments that target cancer’s epigenetic “control system.”

clusters.title: What teens eat could be affecting their mental health more than we thought
clusters.summary: What teens eat might matter more for their mental health than previously thought. A sweeping review of nearly 20 studies found that healthier diets are often linked to fewer depressive symptoms, while poor eating habits may go hand in hand with greater psychological distress. Interestingly, focusing on whole dietary patterns—not just individual nutrients—showed more consistent benefits, suggesting that overall eating habits could play a meaningful role during this critical stage of brain development.
